docs.title: screen-ocr
docs.text:
The screen-ocr repository provides a Python library for performing Optical Character Recognition (OCR) on screen captures. It supports multiple OCR backends, including Tesseract, EasyOCR, WinRT (Windows Runtime), and Talon. The library focuses on being flexible and robust, allowing users to capture screenshots of the entire screen, specific regions, or the current window, and to process these images with their preferred OCR engine.

The main dependencies of the library, specified in pyproject.toml, are:

  • pillow: Used for image manipulation, such as resizing, and converting to grayscale.
  • rapidfuzz: Used for fuzzy string matching.

Other dependencies are optional, and are specified as extras:

  • numpy, pytesseract, pandas, scikit-image: These are used when the tesseract extra is installed and provides support for the Tesseract OCR backend.
  • winsdk: This is used when the winrt extra is installed and provides support for the Windows Runtime OCR backend.
  • easyocr, numpy: These are used when the easyocr extra is installed, and provides support for the EasyOCR backend.
  • imagehash, pytest, scikit-image, scikit-learn: These are used when the dev extra is installed and provide the tools for testing, evaluating, and tuning OCR models.

The repository is structured as follows:

  • The root directory contains configuration files like .python-version, .gitattributes, .gitignore, pyproject.toml, .pre-commit-config.yaml, and uv.lock, as well as the LICENSE, README.md, and a utility script, screen_ocr_tool.py.
  • The tests directory contains tests for the core functionality and utilities for evaluating OCR performance.
  • The .github directory contains configuration for GitHub itself, including GitHub Actions workflows.
  • The screen_ocr directory contains the core logic for the screen-ocr library.

The main functionality of the library is provided in the screen_ocr subdirectory, with the most important files being:

  • screen_ocr/_screen_ocr.py: This file defines the main Reader class and handles tasks such as capturing screenshots, preprocessing images, running OCR using the selected backend, and locating words within the OCR results.
  • screen_ocr/_base.py: Defines the base classes used by all OCR backends, such as OcrWord, OcrLine, OcrResult, and OcrBackend.
  • screen_ocr/_tesseract.py: Implements the Tesseract OCR backend, handling setup, preprocessing, running OCR, and parsing results.
  • screen_ocr/_easyocr.py: Implements the EasyOCR backend.
  • screen_ocr/_winrt.py: Implements the Windows Runtime (WinRT) OCR backend.
  • screen_ocr/_talon.py: Implements the Talon OCR backend.
  • screen_ocr/__init__.py: Makes the library's functionality available when it's imported.
  • screen_ocr/__main__.py: Provides a command-line interface for running OCR.

The tests subdirectory includes the following important test files:

  • tests/screen_ocr_test.py: Contains unit tests for core logic like ScreenContents.find_longest_matching_suffix and ScreenContents._generate_candidates_from_line.
  • tests/test_utils.py: Provides helper functions and classes for testing, including the cost function, and the OcrEstimator class for tuning OCR parameters using scikit-learn.
  • tests/test_utils_test.py: Contains unit tests for the functions in tests/test_utils.py, specifically the cost function.

The .github/workflows subdirectory has a workflow file:

  • github/workflows/deploy_repo_guide.yml: This workflow publishes documentation generated by the repo-guide tool.

The screen_ocr_tool.py is a utility script used for debugging and evaluating OCR performance on a dataset of image and text files in a local folder. It can run in a debug mode to print outputs or perform a grid search for finding optimal parameters for the OcrEstimator class.

These components work together to provide a comprehensive OCR solution, with the core logic separated from the backend-specific implementations. The library prioritizes flexibility by supporting various OCR engines, and maintainability through a solid testing framework.

docs.title: .github
docs.text:
The screen-ocr/.github directory contains configuration for GitHub itself, including workflows.

The workflows subdirectory contains workflow definitions for GitHub Actions. These workflows automate tasks related to the repository.

The workflows/deploy_repo_guide.yml file defines a workflow that publishes documentation generated by the repo-guide tool. This workflow is manually triggered using workflow_dispatch. It checks out the repository, then uses the wolfmanstout/deploy-repo-guide@v1 action to generate and deploy the documentation. The gemini-key input to the action is provided as a secret named LLM_GEMINI_KEY.

docs.title: workflows
docs.text:
The screen-ocr/.github/workflows directory contains workflow definitions for GitHub Actions. These workflows automate tasks related to the repository.

The deploy_repo_guide.yml file, located at https://github.com/wolfmanstout/screen-ocr/blob/master/.github/workflows/deploy_repo_guide.yml, defines a workflow that publishes documentation generated by the repo-guide tool. This workflow is manually triggered using workflow_dispatch. It checks out the repository, then uses the wolfmanstout/deploy-repo-guide@v1 action to generate and deploy the documentation. The gemini-key input to the action is provided as a secret named LLM_GEMINI_KEY.

docs.title: screen_ocr
docs.text:
The screen-ocr/screen_ocr directory contains the core logic for the screen-ocr library, which performs Optical Character Recognition (OCR) on screenshots. It includes modules for handling different OCR backends, processing images, and locating words within the captured text.

Here's a breakdown of the most important files and subdirectories:

  • _screen_ocr.py: This file defines the main Reader class, which is the primary interface for using the library. It handles:

    • Creating readers with different backends (tesseract, easyocr, winrt, and talon).
    • Capturing screenshots, either of the entire screen, a specific region, or the current window.
    • Preprocessing images (resizing, adding margins, and converting to grayscale).
    • Running OCR using the selected backend.
    • Adjusting OCR results to account for image preprocessing.
    • Locating words within the OCR results using fuzzy matching via the ScreenContents class.
    • Providing methods for finding the nearest word to a given location and for finding the longest matching prefix or suffix of a given string.
    • Defining WordLocation and ScreenContents data classes for use by the above methods.

  • _base.py: This file defines the base classes used by all OCR backends. It includes:

    • OcrWord: Represents a single word identified by OCR, along with its bounding box coordinates.
    • OcrLine: Represents a line of text, containing a list of OcrWord objects.
    • OcrResult: Represents the entire OCR result, containing a list of OcrLine objects.
    • OcrBackend: An abstract base class that all OCR backends must inherit from.

  • _tesseract.py: This file implements the Tesseract OCR backend. It handles:

    • Setting up the Tesseract environment, including specifying the data path and command.
    • Preprocessing images, such as applying thresholding to convert the image to black and white.
    • Running Tesseract OCR.
    • Parsing the Tesseract output into an OcrResult.

  • _easyocr.py: This file implements the EasyOCR backend, which is another OCR engine. It handles:

    • Setting up the EasyOCR reader.
    • Running EasyOCR.
    • Converting EasyOCR output to an OcrResult.

  • _winrt.py: This file implements the Windows Runtime (WinRT) OCR backend. It handles:

    • Setting up the WinRT OCR engine in a background thread, as required by WinRT.
    • Running WinRT OCR.
    • Converting WinRT output to an OcrResult.

  • _talon.py: This file implements the Talon OCR backend. It handles:

    • Running Talon OCR.
    • Adjusting bounding boxes, particularly for non-Windows platforms.
    • Converting the Talon OCR output into an OcrResult.

  • __init__.py: This file makes the library's functionality available when it's imported. It imports everything from _screen_ocr.py.

  • __main__.py: This file provides a simple command-line interface for running OCR on the screen.

These files work together to provide a flexible and robust OCR library. The Reader class in _screen_ocr.py acts as a central point for interacting with the library, while the backend-specific modules handle the details of different OCR engines.

docs.title: tests
docs.text:
The screen-ocr/tests directory contains tests for the screen-ocr library. It includes unit tests and utilities for evaluating OCR performance.

  • screen_ocr_test.py contains unit tests for the core logic of screen_ocr. It specifically tests ScreenContents.find_longest_matching_suffix and ScreenContents._generate_candidates_from_line, which are important for the library's text search functionality. The find_longest_matching_suffix test checks how well the function can identify the longest matching suffix of a search string within OCR results. The _generate_candidates_from_line test validates that the function correctly splits a line of OCR text into a list of possible text candidates, which is used for the search algorithm. These tests are critical for ensuring the accuracy and reliability of the text matching features.
  • test_utils.py provides helper functions and classes for testing. The cost function calculates a score based on the fuzzy string match between an OCR result and the ground truth text, with higher scores indicating better matches. The OcrEstimator class is a wrapper around the screen_ocr.Reader that makes it compatible with scikit-learn's machine learning framework. It is used for tuning OCR parameters using grid search. The class creates an screen_ocr.Reader using a variety of configurations for image preprocessing such as thresholding and resizing, making it flexible for different OCR needs.
  • test_utils_test.py contains unit tests for the functions and classes defined in test_utils.py. Currently, it only tests the cost function. It asserts that strings with fewer differences relative to the ground truth have a lower cost.

These files are critical for ensuring the quality and performance of the screen-ocr library, as they provide both the tests for the core functionality and tools for evaluating and tuning OCR models.
